perturbation.original_text: A. You will procure at your expense and maintain in full force and effect during the term of this Agreement, an insurance policy or policies protecting you, us and our designated affiliates, and their officers, directors, partners and employees against any loss, liability, personal injury, death, or property damage or expense whatsoever arising or occurring upon or in connection with the Franchised Restaurant, as we may reasonably require for our own and your protection. We will be named an additional insured in such policy or policies.
perturbation.changed_text: A. You will procure at your expense and maintain in full force and effect during the term of this Agreement, an insurance policy or policies protecting you against any loss, liability, personal injury, death, or property damage or expense whatsoever arising or occurring upon or in connection with the Franchised Restaurant. We will be named an additional insured in such policy or policies.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'us and our designated affiliates, and their officers, directors, partners and employees' from the insurance requirement, the franchisee is no longer explicitly obligated to protect the franchisor and its related parties from liabilities. This could expose the franchisor to significant financial risks in case of lawsuits or damages caused by the franchisee's operations. The explicit requirement to protect the franchisor should be included in insurance, according to standard business practices and potentially state-specific franchise laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of vicarious liability and duty of care in tort law, as well as specific state franchise laws relating to indemnity and insurance.
perturbation.law_explanation: By not requiring the franchisee to maintain insurance that protects the franchisor, this modified clause contradicts the general principles of vicarious liability and duty of care. Section 414 of the Restatement (Second) of Torts indicates a principal can be liable for the negligence of an independent contractor (the franchisee) if they retain control over the work.  Requiring insurance is a key risk management practice, and its omission exposes the franchisor to potential liability for the franchisee’s negligence without insurance coverage.  Many state franchise laws will imply the need for such protection in franchise agreements to protect the reasonable expectations of both parties.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit requirement for the franchisee's insurance policy to protect the franchisor, its affiliates, officers, directors, partners, and employees. This omission creates a significant legal contradiction because it undermines standard business practices and potentially state-specific franchise laws that imply the need for such protection. The law explanation highlights the franchisor's potential vicarious liability for the franchisee's negligence, which the original clause addressed by requiring the franchisee to obtain insurance that also covered the franchisor. The removal of this protection exposes the franchisor to significant financial risks and potential liabilities.

perturbation.original_text: C. The insurance afforded by the policy or policies respecting liability shall not be limited in any way by reason of any insurance which may be maintained by us. The insurance coverage must commence as of the date the location of the Franchised Restaurant has been secured. You agree to deliver to us prior to opening and periodically at any time upon our request, proper certificate evidencing the existence of the insurance coverage which names us as a named insured. Such certificate shall state that said policy or policies will not be canceled or altered without at least twenty (20) days prior written notice to us and shall reflect proof of payment of premiums. Maintenance of such insurance and the performance by you of the obligations under this Paragraph will not relieve you of liability under the indemnity provision set forth in this Agreement. Minimum limits as required above may be modified from time to time, as conditions require, by written notice to you and we may require you to provide periodic proof of payment of premiums.
perturbation.changed_text: C. The insurance afforded by the policy or policies respecting liability shall not be limited in any way by reason of any insurance which may be maintained by us. The insurance coverage must commence as of the date the location of the Franchised Restaurant has been secured. You agree to deliver to us prior to opening proper certificate evidencing the existence of the insurance coverage which names us as a named insured. Maintenance of such insurance and the performance by you of the obligations under this Paragraph will not relieve you of liability under the indemnity provision set forth in this Agreement. Minimum limits as required above may be modified from time to time, as conditions require, by written notice to you and we may require you to provide periodic proof of payment of premiums.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'and periodically at any time upon our request, proper certificate evidencing the existence of the insurance coverage which names us as a named insured. Such certificate shall state that said policy or policies will not be canceled or altered without at least twenty (20) days prior written notice to us and shall reflect proof of payment of premiums', this weakens the franchisor's ability to monitor and enforce the insurance requirements throughout the term of the agreement. Some states will impose regulations for how to maintain Insurance compliance. This creates a legal risk, as the franchisor has reduced assurance that the franchisee is maintaining the required insurance coverage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied duty of good faith and fair dealing; potential violation of state franchise laws related to insurance requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the periodic proof of insurance requirement, this modified clause undermines the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.  While the UCC primarily deals with the sale of goods, its principle of good faith (UCC § 1-304) is widely applied to contracts in general. The franchisor's reasonable expectation is that the franchisee will continuously maintain insurance as agreed. Eliminating the mechanism to verify this ongoing compliance introduces an element of bad faith, as the franchisor now has no reliable way to ensure the franchisee is fulfilling a critical contractual obligation related to risk management. Certain state franchise laws might also mandate ongoing insurance compliance and verification, making this omission a potential violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification omits the requirement for periodic proof of insurance, weakening the franchisor's ability to ensure ongoing compliance, which relates to the implied duty of good faith. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state that the change introduces a legal risk by reducing assurance of maintained insurance coverage and potentially undermining the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Snippet 1 states, "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." By removing the requirement for periodic verification of insurance, the modified contract arguably allows the franchisee to potentially breach their duty to maintain adequate insurance, a vital aspect of risk management within the franchise agreement. The definition of Omission Legal Contradiction states 'A legal contradiction in this category happens when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant,' further suggesting a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: B. The policy or policies shall be written by an insurance company satisfactory to us in accordance with standards and specifications set forth in the Manuals or otherwise in writing, and shall include, at a minimum (except as different coverages and policy limits may reasonably be specified for all franchisees from time to time by us in the Manuals or otherwise in writing) the following: 1. All risks coverage insurance on the Franchised Restaurant and all fixtures, equipment, supplies and other property used in the operation of the Franchised Restaurant, for full repair and replacement value of the machinery, equipment, improvements and betterments, without any applicable co-insurance clause, except that an appropriate deductible clause shall be permitted. 2. Worker's compensation and employer's liability insurance as well as such other insurance as may be required by statute or rule of the state in which the Franchised Restaurant is located and operated. 3. Comprehensive general liability insurance and product liability insurance with minimum limits of ONE MILLION Dollars ($1,000,000) combined single limit including the following coverages: contractual liability; personal injury; products/completed operation; and tenant's fire legal liability; insuring against all claims, suits, obligations, liabilities and damages, including attorneys' fees, based upon or arising out of actual or alleged personal injuries or property damage resulting from, or occurring in the course of, or on or about or otherwise relating to the Franchised Restaurant, provided that the required amounts herein may be modified from time to time by us to reflect inflation or future experience with claims. 4. If you offer delivery service or utilize motor vehicles for any other purpose in the operation of the Franchised Restaurant, automobile liability insurance, including owned, hired and non-owned vehicle coverage, with a combined single limit of at least ONE MILLION Dollars ($1,000,000). 5. Such insurance and types of coverage as may be required by the terms of any lease for the Franchised Restaurant, or as may be required from time to time by us. 6. Liquor liability coverage in a minimum amount of ONE MILLION Dollars ($1,000,000) or such other amount as may be specified by us.
perturbation.changed_text: B. The policy or policies shall be written by an insurance company satisfactory to us in accordance with standards and specifications set forth in the Manuals or otherwise in writing, and shall include the following: 1. Insurance on the Franchised Restaurant. 2. Worker's compensation and employer's liability insurance. 3. General liability insurance and product liability insurance. 4. If you offer delivery service or utilize motor vehicles for any other purpose in the operation of the Franchised Restaurant, automobile liability insurance. 5. Such insurance and types of coverage as may be required by the terms of any lease for the Franchised Restaurant, or as may be required from time to time by us. 6. Liquor liability coverage.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting explicit specifications such as 'All risks coverage,' 'full repair and replacement value,' specific coverage types (contractual liability, personal injury, etc.), and minimum limits (ONE MILLION Dollars), and automobile insurance including owned, hired and non-owned vehicle coverage, and liquor liability coverage in a minimum amount of ONE MILLION Dollars, the contract introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance with insurance regulations. States often have rules about the minimum coverage required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of contract related to reasonable business expectations; potential violations of state-specific insurance regulations for businesses.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing specifics about coverage types and limits potentially violates implicit expectations within a franchise agreement and may contradict state insurance laws. For example, New York Insurance Law § 3420 mandates certain minimum liability coverage in insurance policies. Omitting these details allows the franchisee to obtain minimal coverage that might be insufficient to protect the franchisor from liabilities, directly contradicting the implied reasonable business expectations and possibly violating state law if the resulting policy fails to meet minimum requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - Unofficial New York Codes, Rules and Regulations This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies minimum limits and coverage types for insurance, while the changed text omits these specifics, creating ambiguity about the required coverage. The Law Explanation suggests this omission could violate state insurance laws by allowing insufficient coverage. While specific New York laws could not be verified with the provided scraped snippets, the Perturbation Explanation emphasizes that states often have rules about minimum coverage, and the Omission Legal Contradiction definition indicates that removing legally mandated consumer protections constitutes a legal contradiction. The omission of key insurance requirements undermines the franchisor's protection and potentially violates implied business expectations and state law, supporting a finding of contradiction.
